comment_text.sn17.1:2.1: Dāruṇa is “grim”, fearful and unyielding as tough wood; kaṭuka is “bitter” like the taste, or like stabbing pain; pharusa is “harsh” like a prickly bush or abusive speech.

root_text.sn17.1:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn17.1:2.1: “Dāruṇo, bhikkhave, lābhasakkārasiloko kaṭuko pharuso antarāyiko anuttarassa yogakkhemassa adhigamāya.
root_text.sn17.1:2.3: ‘uppannaṁ lābhasakkārasilokaṁ pajahissāma, na ca no uppanno lābhasakkārasiloko cittaṁ pariyādāya ṭhassatī’ti.

translation_text.sn17.1:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn17.1:1.3: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants,
translation_text.sn17.1:2.1: “Possessions, honor, and popularity are grim, bitter, and harsh. They’re an obstacle to reaching the supreme sanctuary from the yoke.
translation_text.sn17.1:2.2: So you should train like this:
translation_text.sn17.1:2.3: ‘We will give up arisen possessions, honor, and popularity, and we won’t let them occupy our minds.’
